coredata.dc:title: Diaminodiphenyl sulfone as a novel ligand for synthesis of gold nanoparticles for simultaneous colorimetric assay of three trivalent metal cations (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+)
coredata.dc:creator: Meena, Raveena
Mehta, Vaibhavkumar N.
Bhamore, Jigna R.
Rao, Potnuru Tirumala
Park, Tae-Jung
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
coredata.dc:description: A novel ligand namely diaminodiphenyl sulfone (DDS) is introduced for the fabrication of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) without use of external reducing agent. Various parameters (reagents concentrations and time) were investigated for the fabrication of DDS-AuNPs with controlled size and color. The DDS ligand chemistry on AuNPs plays a vital role to act AuNPs as three trivalent metal cations sensor i.e., Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+. By modifying the surface chemistry of AuNPs with DDS, DDS-AuNPs acted as trivalent metal ions sensor, resulting a drastic plasmonic shift in the absorption spectra and change in color from red to blue. The spectroscopic and electron microscopic data revealed that three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+) were greatly induced the spectral shifts and morphological changes in the DDS-AuNPs, demonstrating the aggregation of DDS-AuNPs. Rapid colorimetric assay of three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+) is achieved in the wider range (1.0–500 μM) and the detection limits of DDS-AuNPs sensor were 0.62, 0.69 and 0.78 μM for Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions, respectively. Moreover, this method possessed quantitative detection ability for three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+) with good recognition, and reproducibility, representing great potentiality in assaying of three metal ions in real samples (river, tap and canal water).
coredata.dcterms:subject: DDS-AuNPs
Three trivalent metal ions
UV–visible spectrometry
Electron microscopy
Environmental samples
Colorimetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: DDS as a new ligand for AuNPs synthesis
DDS-AuNPs as a sensor for Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ions
Analysis of three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+) in water samples
Role of Au3+ ion, DDS and NaOH concentrations
Colorimetric assay of three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+)
Effect of NaCl on the sensitivity
Analysis of three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+) using DDS-AuNPs as a sensor

Department of Science and Technology, Government of Kerala

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India under Fast-Track Young Scientist Scheme (No. SR/FT/CS-54/2010). SKK thanks the Director, SVNIT, Surat for providing necessary facilities to carry out this work.

This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology , Government of India under Fast-Track Young Scientist Scheme (No. SR/FT/CS-54/2010 ). SKK thanks the Director, SVNIT, Surat for providing necessary facilities to carry out this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Effect of DDS concentration ranging from 1.0 to 1.75 mM on AuNPs (inset: color of DDS-AuNPs solution) and (b) effect of NaOH concentration (0.10–0.225 M) on the UV–visible spectra of AuNPs using DDS at 1.25 mM. (Inset: color of DDS-AuNPs solution.)
TEM images of DDS-AuNPs at (a) 20 nm and (inset: particle size distribution histogram of DDS-AuNPs) (b) 2.0 nm scale bar. DDS-AuNPs with addition of (c) Al3+ and (d) of Cr3+ ions.

Investigation of DDS-AuNPs as a novel probe for the development of metal ions sensor (Co2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Na+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Fe3+, 1 mM). Inset: visual changes of DDS-AuNPs with introduction of different metal ions.

UV–visible absorption spectra of DDS-AuNPs in presence of 0.2 M NaCl with (a) Al3+ ion (1.0 μM–30 μM). Inset: visual changes of DDS-AuNPs with Al3+ ion and (b) Fe3+ ion (5.0 μM–500 μM). Inset: visual changes of DDS-AuNPs with Fe3+ ion at Tris-HCl pH 5.0.
UV–visible absorption spectra of DDS-AuNPs in presence of 0.2 M NaCl with Cr3+ ion (5.0 μM–500 μM). Inset: visual changes of DDS-AuNPs with Cr3+ ion at Tris-HCl pH 5.0.

UV–visible absorption spectra of DDS-AuNPs with three trivalent metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions; 500 μM) with addition of other metal ions (Co2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+; 1.0 mM). Inset: visual changes of DDS-AuNPs with metal ions.
Schematic representaition for aggregation of DDS-AuNPs induced by three trivalent metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+)

head.ce:title: Diaminodiphenyl sulfone as a novel ligand for synthesis of gold nanoparticles for simultaneous colorimetric assay of three trivalent metal cations (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Applied Chemistry Department, S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat 395007, Gujarat, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: S. V. National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Applied Chemistry Department, S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat – 395007, Gujarat, India

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: ASPEE Shakilam Biotechnology Institute, Navsari Agricultural University, Surat 395007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Institute of Interdisciplinary Convergence Research, Research Institute of Halal Industrialization Technology, Chung-Ang University, 84 Heukseok-ro, Dongjak-gu, Seoul 06974, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute of Halal Industrialization Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Institute of Interdisciplinary Convergence Research, Research Institute of Halal Industrialization Technology, Chung-Ang University, 84 Heukseok-ro, Dongjak-gu, Seoul, 06974, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel ligand namely diaminodiphenyl sulfone (DDS) is introduced for the fabrication of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) without use of external reducing agent. Various parameters (reagents concentrations and time) were investigated for the fabrication of DDS-AuNPs with controlled size and color. The DDS ligand chemistry on AuNPs plays a vital role to act AuNPs as three trivalent metal cations sensor i.e., Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+. By modifying the surface chemistry of AuNPs with DDS, DDS-AuNPs acted as trivalent metal ions sensor, resulting a drastic plasmonic shift in the absorption spectra and change in color from red to blue. The spectroscopic and electron microscopic data revealed that three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+) were greatly induced the spectral shifts and morphological changes in the DDS-AuNPs, demonstrating the aggregation of DDS-AuNPs. Rapid colorimetric assay of three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+) is achieved in the wider range (1.0–500 μM) and the detection limits of DDS-AuNPs sensor were 0.62, 0.69 and 0.78 μM for Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions, respectively. Moreover, this method possessed quantitative detection ability for three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+) with good recognition, and reproducibility, representing great potentiality in assaying of three metal ions in real samples (river, tap and canal water).

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, various anisotropic gold nanostructures (nanoparticles, nanorods, nanoflowers and nanocubes) have been widely used as colorimetric probes for the detection of variety of analytes [1]. Among them, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been evolved as reliable visual nanoindicators for the detection of multiple analytes (drugs, pesticides, biomolecules, metal ions, and anions) with significant analytical merits (high selectivity, rapidity, simplicity and sensitivity) [2–8]. Notably, AuNPs exhibit outstanding optical features and physico-chemical properties, exploring them to act as remarkable agents for naked-eye detection of various chemical species [9–12]. Usually, “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches have been introduced for the production of AuNPs, however “bottom-up” has proven to be simple and rapid route for fabrication of AuNPs with controlled size and molecular architectures [7,8]. Based on the growth mechanism, “bottom-up” is usually classified into “seed-growth” and “in-situ” methods, where seed-growth approach involves two steps (nucleation and growth), whereas “in-situ” involves one-step to fabricate AuNPs [11]. Furthermore, plasmonic nanoparticles (AuNPs, Ag NPs and Cu NPs) and bi-metallic core/shell nanoparticles (Au@Ag, Au/AgI) have been widely used as visual indicators for the analysis of target analytes, which is relatively dependent on the “surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect” [13,14].

To fabricate AuNPs with new analytical applications, numerous organic molecules such as tyrosine [15,16], gallic acid [17], pyrogallol acid [18], carbon dots [19], alkanethiol [20], histidine [21], N-acetylglucosamine [22], ampicillin [23], 4-amino antipyrine [24], 4-amino nicotinic acid [25], quercetin [26], dicoumarol [27] and 2,3,4-trihydroxy benzophenone [28] have been introduced as novel ligands for the formation of metal (Au, Ag and Cu) nanomaterials with new surface chemistry, favoring to establish naked-eye colorimetric methods for the detection of trace level target molecules. To tune the selectivity of AuNPs, thiourea [29] and ytterbium [30] have been functionalized on the surfaces of AuNPs for label-free colorimetric detection of Hg2+ ion and organophosphorus pesticides in environmental samples. In summary of the above methods, as-fabricated AuNPs are well dispersed within 15–25 nm diameter range and effectively used as novel analytical sensor for the detection of various molecules, which confirms that the introduction of new ligand chemistry in AuNPs synthesis exhibits dual functions i.e., template and reducing agent. Thus, the introduction of new ligand in AuNPs synthesis would possess unique molecular architectures on the surface of AuNPs for novel sensing applications.

Although metal ions are found to be key elements for various living systems, excess amounts of metal ions in ecology could inhibits various biopathways in the living organs. Three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions) have shown to be most recognizable elements in various biological and environmental samples due to either their remarkable metabolic applications in living system or toxic nature towards various organs [31,32]. For example, Al3+ ion may disturb the regular functions of organs due to its interaction ability with important biomolecules (casein proteins, thrombocytes and amyloid peptides) [31]. Fe3+ ion exhibits specific redox and chelation ability that allows to acts as a key and essential element for numerous cell and organ functions [33]. Similarly, Cr3+ ion is one of the essential trace element for many biochemical reactions in the cells [34]. However, their abnormal levels cause serious disorders (Al3+ ion - bone softening, Alzheimer's and Parkinson's dieses; Fe3+ ion - renal failure, and anemia; Cr3+ ion – DNA damage) in humans [34–37]. Thus, various atomic spectroscopic techniques have been applied for the analysis of various metal species including three trivalent metal ions from various samples [38–42]. Even though these atomic spectroscopic techniques exhibited higher sensitivity for trivalent metal ions analysis, unfortunately they required tedious sample pre-treatment procedures and more time, which will hamper their wider use in analytical chemistry for rapid analysis of trivalent metal ions. Thus, it is necessary to establish a simple analytical platform for the simultaneous analysis of three metal ions in various environment media. The key challenge in the AuNPs synthesis is to functionalize AuNPs surfaces with additional ligands on their surfaces which requires multiple steps. To overcome this issue, diaminodiphenyl sulfone (DDS) also called as dapsone is introduced as a novel ligand for the synthesis of AuNPs, allowing to generate DDS-AuNPs with new analytical sensing applications.

In the current study, first time, diaminodiphenyl sulfone (DDS) is used as a novel ligand for the fabrication of AuNPs without any additional reagents. The as-synthesized DDS-AuNPs exhibited SPR peak at 528 nm and showed red color, which confirms that the radius of DDS-AuNPs is in the range of 10–15 nm. The DDS provides new ligand chemistry on AuNPs, allowing them to act as trivalent metal ions sensor (Scheme 1 ). The red color of DDS-AuNPs become blue color upon the addition of three trivalent target metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+and Cr3+ ions), causing specific spectral shift in the SPR peak of DDS-AuNPs from 528 to 551, 560 and 558 nm for Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions, respectively. Based on this, an analytical assay is carried out for the detection of three metal ions i.e., Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions, providing a rapid and simple portable analytical platform for assaying of three trivalent metal ions from various samples with good precision and accuracy.

HAuCl4·xH2O, tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris buffer), Metal ion salts (Ba2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Na+) and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich, Saint Louis, MO, USA. DDS was received as gift sample from Torrent Pharmaceuticals Ltd., Ahmedabad, Gujarat, India. Deionized water was used for all the experiments, which is obtained from Milli-Q Ultrapure Water Purified System with 18.0 MΩ·cm.

Absorption spectral characteristics of DDS-AuNPs were recorded in aqueous media on a Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer, Ocean Optics, USA. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained from FT-IR spectroscopy (Shimadzu FT-IR 8400S, Japan). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were recorded on Tecnai 20 (Philips, Holland).

ce:section.ce:section-title: DDS as a new ligand for AuNPs synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical synthesis, 9.0 mL of auric chloride (0.5 mM) solution was taken into a reaction flask and then heated until rigorous bubbles (80–90 °C) at 700 rpm. To this, 1.0 mL of 1.25 mM DDS and 1.0 mL of NaOH (0.15 M) were added simultaneously to the above solution and then stirred for 5.0 min, yielding a remarkable change in color from light yellow to red color, which confirms that the formation of DDS-AuNPs. The effect DDS concentration and time were investigated by measuring the absorption spectra of DDS-AuNPs. The formed DDS-AuNPs were stored in refrigerator at 4 °C for further characterization and sensing application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DDS-AuNPs as a sensor for Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ions
ce:section.ce:para: UV–visible spectrophotometric detections of three target metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions) were proceeded by using DDS-AuNPs as a trivalent nanosensor. Typically, 1.0 mL of DDS-AuNPs solution was taken into a series of glass vials and then 0.5 mL of different concentrations of three trivalent metal ions (1.0 μM–30.0 μM for Al3+, 5.0 μM–500 μM for Fe3+ and 5.0 μM–500 μM for Cr3+ ions) were added independently into the above vials and then vortexed for 2 min. The UV–visible spectral characteristics of the above solutions were recorded in the range of 400–900 nm. The magnitudes of absorbance ratios at A551 nm/A528 nm, A560 nm/A528 nm and A558 nm/A528 nm were used as index parameters for colorimetric assaying of Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+) in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To analyze three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+) in real samples, water samples were collected from Tapi river, Surat, Gujarat, tap water and canal water. The analysis was carried out by the standard addition procedure. Typically, the collected water samples were treated with known concentrations of three target metal ions (Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions - 5.0, 25.0 and 75.0 μM and Al3+ ion – 1.0, 15.0 and 30.0 μM) and then the actual amounts of three trivalent metal ions were estimated by the earlier described procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of Au3+ ion, DDS and NaOH concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: First time, DDS is introduced as a promising ligand for the fabrication of AuNPs with controlled color and size. During the growth of AuNPs, the color of chloroauric acid is changed from pale yellow to colorless and then red/blue, demonstrating the formed DDS-AuNPs have different SPR characteristics. Thus, the control experiments (effect of Au3+ ion, and DDS concentrations) were investigated to clarify the role of DDS on the growth of AuNPs. It is in-situ synthetic approach since the reduction of Au3+ ion to Au0 atoms generates nuclei, which results the growth of AuNPs via nucleation and growth. The formed AuNPs surfaces were effectively protected by the DDS, revealing the dual role of DDS i.e., reducing and capping properties.

To estimate the optimum concentration of DDS, different molar ratios of Au3+ ion:DDS (1:10, 1:12.5, 1:15 and 1:17.5 mM) were investigated for the synthesis of DDS-AuNPs (Fig. 1a). The DDS-AuNPs solutions exhibit SPR peaks at 543, 528, 542 and 541 nm (colors - purple, red and blue) using molar ratios of Au3+ion:DDS at 1:10, 1:12.5, 1:15 and 1:17.5. In comparison, the AuNPs were synthesized with good SPR peak at 528 nm and red color when the molar ratio of Au3+ ion:DDS is at 1:12.5 mM. When the molar ratio of Au3+ ion: DDS is higher, the color of AuNPs changes into purple and blue, which is eventually proceeded to AuNPs aggregation. However, the molar ratio at 1:12.5 mM provides the best absorption spectrum of DDS-AuNPs with SPR peak at 528 nm. Furthermore, the effect of NaOH concentration (0.1–0.225 M) was investigated on the SPR spectra and color of DDS-AuNPs by keeping the above optimal conditions (Fig. 1b). The absorbance of SPR band of DDS-AuNPs decreases with increasing NaOH concentration, which reveals that the colloidal AuNPs are aggregated at high NaOH concentration. This absorption characteristics revealed that the concentration of NaOH is one of the key element in synthesizing well dispersed DDS-AuNPs. Thus, we selected 0.15 M of NaOH as an optimal concentration for the formation of DDS-AuNPs.

The formation of nanosize DDS-AuNPs was confirmed by appearing strong SPR absorption peak at 528nm using 0.5 mM of Au3+ ion and 1.25 mM of DDS in the presence of 0.15 M NaOH. Basically, surface plasmon resonance peak generates from the oscillation of free electrons on the AuNPs surfaces, yielding the appearance of absorption peak in the visible region, which corresponds to the vibration frequencies of AuNPs. Thus, the origin of SPR peak at 528 nm confirms that the formation of AuNPs with red color (inset of Fig. 1), which represents that the formed DDS-AuNPs are ~10 nm range with spherical shape [43]. However, the diameter of DDS-AuNPs was estimated by TEM. Fig. 2a–b represents the TEM image of DDS-AuNPs and the average size of DDS-AuNPs is 7.5 ± 2.5 nm. These results demonstrated that AuNPs were successfully formed by using DDS as a new ligand with an average size of 7.5 ± 2.5 nm.

FT-IR spectra of DDS and DDS-AuNPs were studied and shown in Fig. S1 of Supporting information. The FT-IR spectrum of DDS exhibited well resolved peaks at 3362.04 cm−1, 1591.33 cm−1 and 1498.74 cm−1, which are ascribed to the NH2 group asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations. Noticeably, the peak at 1907.66 cm−1 belongs to the primary amine of DDS. The peaks at 1631, 1282, 1143, 831, 692 and 551 cm−1 are corresponded to the (NH)b, (S=O)s, (CN)s, (CH)b-in plane, (CH)b out of plane and (S=O)b frequencies of DDS. However, the FT-IR spectral characteristics of DDS were completely changed due to its oxidation to form DDS-AuNPs. Importantly, the intensity of -NH2 group of DDS was drastically decreased due to its reducing ability to reduce Au3+ ion to Au0 atoms, which yields to form DDS-AuNPs. This spectral study revealed that DDS acts as unique novel ligand for the fabrication of DDS-AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric assay of three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+)
ce:section.ce:para: The main objective of this method is to develop a facile and rapid analytical tool for colorimetric assay of multiple analytes without aid of sophisticated instrumentation at microvolume samples. The colorimetric assay of three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions) was conducted by using DDS-AuNPs as a trivalent metal ions nanosensor. With introduction of tri-, and di-valent metal ions (Al3+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mn2+ and Hg2+), DDS-AuNPs solution exhibits an obvious color change from red to blue only with three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions) (Fig. 3 ). The treatment of DDS-AuNPs solution with divalent metal ions (Co2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mn2+ and Hg2+) did not show any obvious color change. The color of DDS-AuNPs is changed due to the drastic changes in the dispersion state of DDS-AuNPs, which is due to the addition of three metal ions. This interesting behavior is due the generation of target metal ions complexes with DDS-AuNPs. Therefore, the changes in the absorbance and color of DDS-AuNPs may be due to the formation of Al3+/Fe3+/Cr3+ ion-DDS-AuNPs complexes, revealing the DDS-AuNPs can be used as visual indicator for the rapid colorimetric assay of three metal ions.

The DDS contains two primary amine groups and one SO2 group, and the oxidized DDS was effectively attached with the AuNPs surfaces. Based on the spectral data, the formed DDS-AuNPs contains SO2 group outside that may preferentially interact with trivalent metal ions. Therefore, the exposure of three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions) resulted red shifts in the SPR peak of DDS-AuNPs from 528 nm to 551, 560 and 558 nm for Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions, respectively. The remarkable red-shift in the SPR spectra and the color change of DDS-AuNPs are attributed to the formation of DDS-AuNPs-trivalent metal ion nanocomplexes (aggregation). A possible sensing mechanism for the recognition of three trivalent metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+) are shown in Scheme 1. It can be noticed that DDS reduces Au3+ ion and assembled on AuNPs. The dense –SO2 and –NH groups of DDS-AuNPs have ability to interact with three trivalent metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+), resulting to aggregate DDS-AuNPs. Since three trivalent metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+) are strongly coordinated with nitrogen and oxygen atoms of DDS-AuNPs. According to the literature reports [44–46], the remarkable changes were noticed in the NMR and IR spectral characteristics of functional groups (-SO2 and –NH) when trivalent three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+) added to organic ligands containing –SO2 and –NH groups. Thus, we believe that the functional groups (–SO2 and –NH) of DDS-AuNPs play key role to selective coordinate with three trivalent metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+), yielding to form large DDS-AuNPs-trivalent metal ions nanoaggregates, which causes remarkable changes in the absorption spectra of DDS-AuNPs. To ensure the formation of DDS-AuNPs-trivalent metal ions nanoaggregates, TEM images of DDS-AuNPs were investigated with the addition of Al3+ and Cr3+ metal ions (Fig. 2c–d). The well dispersed DDS-AuNPs are aggregated with Al3+ and Cr3+ metal ions, signifying the formation of metal ion complexes with surface groups of DDS-AuNPs, which results the minimization of interparticle distance of DDS-AuNPs. The proximity in the DDS-AuNPs distance resulted to induce the coupling of SPR, yielding a remarkable red-shift (bathochromic shift) in the absorption spectra of DDS-AuNPs and a visual change from red to blue color. The use of DDS as a novel ligand provides metal-binding sides on the surfaces of AuNPs, allowing specific interaction with three metal ions. The metal binding with surface chemistry of DDS-AuNPs leads to a drastic change in the properties (SPR, redox behavior and conductivity) of Au core [47], favoring for the formation of highly nanoaggregates, which could be useful to develop a portable sensor for visual detection of three metal ions.

The Tris-HCl buffer pH dependence absorption spectra of three metal ions induced aggregations of DDS-AuNPs were assessed and the corresponding spectra are shown in Fig. S2a–c of Supporting information. These results revealed that DDS-AuNPs acted as promising colorimetric reader for Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions during the Tris-HCl pH range from 2.0 to 12.0, illustrating DDS-AuNPs could be a suitable visual sensor for recognition of three trivalent metal ions at Tris-HCl pH 5.0, 6.0 and 5.0 for Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of NaCl on the sensitivity
ce:section.ce:para: The salt concentration plays vital role to improve the sensitivity of analyte as it leads to the change in electrostatic interactions between analyte and DDS-AuNPs. To study the effect of ionic strength on sensitivity, various concentration of NaCl (0.1 to 1.0 M) was added to the DDS-AuNPs. The SPR peak of DDS-AuNPs remained unchanged in the presence of NaCl concentration 0.1 and 0.2 M (Supporting information of Fig. S3). Whereas, the higher amount of NaCl (0.3 to 1.0 M) leads to aggregation of DDS-AuNPs due to the decrease in the electrostatic repulsion between neighboring AuNPs. The DDS-AuNPs aggregations resulted a noticeable change in the color from red to blue, leading to a red-shift of SPR peak from 528 to 570 nm. Therefore, 0.2 M NaCl concentration is used to improve the sensitivity of probe towards the Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+, respectively.

The analytical sensitivity of DDS-AuNPs towards three trivalent metal ions was investigated by constructing calibration graph between absorption ratios and concentrations of three target metal ions. To this, the absorption spectra of DDS-AuNPs were measured with various concentrations of three trivalent metal ions (Al3+ ion – 1.0 μM–30.0 μM; Fe3+ ion – 5.0 μM–500 μM and Cr3+ ion – 5.0 μM–500 μM) in the presence of 0.2 M of NaCl (Figs. 4a–b and 5
). Upon addition of three trivalent metal ions separately, the characteristic absorption peak at 528 nm is progressively quenched with increasing concentrations of three trivalent metal ions, yielding new peaks at 551, 560 and 558 nm for Al3+, Fe3+ (Fig. 4a–b) and Cr3+ (Fig. 5) ions, respectively. The absorption ratios at A551nm/A528nm, A560nm/A528nm and A558nm/A528nm showed good linearities in the range of 1.0–22 μM for Al3+ ion and 5.0–100 μM for Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions (Supporting information of Fig. S4). As a result, the regression equations are A551nm/A528nm = 0.003c + 0.919 (R 2 = 0.9909), A560nm/A528nm = 0.001c + 0.888 (R 2 = 0.9665) and A558nm/A528nm = 0.001c + 0.9173 (R 2 = 0.9431) for Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions, respectively. The detection limits are 0.62, 0.69 and 0.78 μM for Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions (S/N = 3). Insets of Figs. 4 and 5 represent the visual color changes of DDS-AuNPs with different concentrations of three trivalent metal ions, revealing the red color of DDS-AuNPs is changed to blue color, which favors the development of label-free portable colorimetric sensor for screening of three trivalent metal ions without aid of advance analytical instruments.

To examine the DDS-AuNPs sensing selectivity towards three trivalent metal ions, three target metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions, 500 μM) were introduced separately into glass vials that contain DDS-AuNPs and then various interfering metal cations (Co2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mn2+ and Hg2+; 1.0 mM) were added separately and the corresponding absorption spectra were measured (Fig. 6 ). Noticeably, DDS-AuNPs are selectively recognized three metal ions Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions in the mixture of interfering species, revealing the DDS-AuNPs could be used as a selective probe for colorimetric assay of three metal ions.

To use DDS-AuNPs as a specific sensor for assaying of each target metal ion in the presence of remaining two target metal ions, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), ascorbic acid (AA) and 3-mercapto-1, 2-propanediol (MPD) were used as masking agents for Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions. As shown in Supporting information of Fig. S5, EDTA was successfully inhibited the inference of Al3+ ion, whereas AA and MPD were also effectively inhibited the interferences of Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions. Further, to avoid possible interference of other two trivalent target metal ions during the single trivalent metal ion sensing, we studied the changes in the absorption spectra of DDS-AuNPs by adding two masking agents and two trivalent metal ions with and without single target trivalent metal ion (Supporting information of Fig. S6). During the Al3+ ion sensing, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions interferences are masked by AA and MPD and in Fe3+ ion sensing, Al3+ and Cr3+ ions are masked by EDTA and MPD. To sense Cr3+ ion in the presence of other two trivalent metal ions (Al3+ ion and Fe3+) EDTA and AA are used as masking agents for Al3+ and Fe3+ ions, which facilitate to sense singe trivalent target metal ion in the presence of remain two trivalent metal ions. These results clearly reveled that DDS-AuNPs could use selective colorimetric sensor for parallel recognition of each trivalent metal ion in the presence of other two trivalent metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+) using DDS-AuNPs as a sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the versatility practical application of DDS-AuNPs as a sensor for three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+), the analysis of three trivalent metal ions were carried out by spiking of different concentrations of three metal ions (Cr3+ and Fe3+ ions - 5.0, 25.0 and 75.0 μM and Al3+ ion – 1.0, 15.0 and 30.0 μM) into collected water samples Tapi river, tap and canal water. The concentration of trivalent metal ions was estimated by the described procedure earlier. The analytical data for the analysis of three trivalent metal ions from spiked water samples are shown in Supporting information of Tables S1–S3. The colorimetric assay of three trivalent metal ions from water samples is satisfactory. The recoveries were found in the range of 97.21–105% with relative standard deviation <5.50%, signifying the DDS-AuNPs-based colorimetric method exhibited good accuracy and precision. Thus, DDS-AuNPs could be used as a colorimetric probe for simultaneous analysis of three trivalent metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+) in water samples with a simple UV–visible spectrometer at minimal volume of samples.

In summary, we explored herein DDS as a novel ligand for the preparation of AuNPs, which acted as selective three metal ions sensor (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+) over other metal ions. The as-fabricated DDS-AuNPs showed characteristic SPR peak at 528 nm and shifted to 551 nm, 560 nm and 558 nm upon the addition of three metal ions (Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+), causing significant color change from red to blue, which could lead to develop a portable analytical device for assaying of three metal ions with naked eye. This probe exhibited detection limits 0.62, 0.69 and 0.78 μM for Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions, respectively. Importantly, EDTA, AA and MPD were used as masking agents for Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions, which could prove that DDS-AuNPs could be used as a sensor for parallel detection of three trivalent metal ions. The DDS-AuNPs can serve as promising three trivalent metal ions sensor for the analysis of Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ions in real samples.

Raveena Meena: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Vaibhavkumar N. Mehta: Investigation. Jigna R. Bhamore: Formal analysis. Potnuru Tirumala Rao: Investigation. Tae-Jung Park: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Suresh Kumar Kailasa: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interest or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India under Fast-Track Young Scientist Scheme (No. SR/FT/CS-54/2010). SKK thanks the Director, SVNIT, Surat for providing necessary facilities to carry out this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2020.113409.
